title: The old tree our street fought to keep
summary: Explain how a neighbourhood worked together to save one old tree the city had marked to cut down.
topic: How a community saved one old tree from being cut down
prompt: Write 9–11 sentences explaining how a group of people saved something small but loved (an old tree, a fountain, a mural). Explain the threat, what people did, and the result. Use past simple, present perfect for the lasting result, and connectors (so, after, because).
hintStarters: Last spring the city put a red mark on the old tree, which meant…
At first only a few neighbours noticed, but after one of them…
We collected signatures and wrote a letter because we believed…
The tree is still standing today, and every summer it…
sampleText: Last spring the city painted a red mark on the old tree at the end of our street in our small town in Mexico. The mark meant they planned to cut it down to make the road wider. The tree was more than a hundred years old, and it gave shade to the bus stop, the fruit seller, and three small houses. At first only a few neighbours noticed, but after one of them knocked on every door, more people came outside to look. We did not want to lose the tree, so we decided to act together. We collected signatures and wrote a polite letter to the council, because we believed the tree was worth saving. An old woman explained that her own mother had played under the same branches as a child. A young engineer drew a simple plan that showed how the road could turn slightly and keep the tree. The council read our letter and, after two meetings, they agreed to change the design. The tree is still standing today, and every summer it gives the same wide shade. We learned that a small group can protect something old if they act early and stay calm. One tree may seem like a tiny thing, but a street without it would never feel the same.

chúng tôi quyết định cùng hành động
chúng tôi thu thập chữ ký

sampleAnnotations.phrase: we believed the tree was worth saving
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: chúng tôi tin rằng cây đáng được giữ lại

sampleAnnotations.phrase: had played under the same branches
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: đã từng chơi dưới những cành cây ấy

sampleAnnotations.phrase: showed how the road could turn slightly

sampleAnnotations.phrase: The tree is still standing today

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Past perfect inside a past story
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu 'her own mother had played under the same branches' dùng quá khứ hoàn thành vì việc người mẹ chơi dưới gốc cây xảy ra trước khoảnh khắc bà cụ kể chuyện trong quá khứ.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Her own mother had played under the same branches' uses past perfect because the mother's playing happened before the past moment when the woman told the story.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect / present for the lasting result
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu 'The tree is still standing today' dùng hiện tại tiếp diễn để cho thấy kết quả vẫn còn đúng đến nay. Sau cả một câu chuyện ở quá khứ, hiện tại đánh dấu điều còn lại.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'The tree is still standing today' uses the present to show the result is still true now. After a story in the past, the present marks what remains.

sampleTranslationVi: Mùa xuân năm ngoái thành phố sơn một dấu đỏ lên cây cổ thụ ở cuối con phố của chúng tôi tại một thị trấn nhỏ ở Mexico. Dấu đó có nghĩa là họ định chặt hạ nó để mở rộng con đường. Cái cây đã hơn một trăm tuổi, và nó cho bóng râm cho trạm xe buýt, người bán trái cây, và ba ngôi nhà nhỏ. Lúc đầu chỉ vài hàng xóm để ý, nhưng sau khi một người trong số họ gõ cửa từng nhà, nhiều người ra ngoài để xem. Chúng tôi không muốn mất cái cây, nên chúng tôi quyết định cùng hành động. Chúng tôi thu thập chữ ký và viết một lá thư lịch sự cho hội đồng, vì chúng tôi tin rằng cây đáng được giữ lại. Một bà cụ giải thích rằng chính mẹ bà đã từng chơi dưới những cành cây ấy khi còn nhỏ. Một kỹ sư trẻ vẽ một bản kế hoạch đơn giản cho thấy con đường có thể uốn nhẹ và giữ lại cái cây. Hội đồng đọc lá thư của chúng tôi và, sau hai cuộc họp, họ đồng ý thay đổi thiết kế. Cây vẫn còn đứng đó hôm nay, và mỗi mùa hè nó cho cùng một mảng bóng râm rộng. Chúng tôi học được rằng một nhóm nhỏ có thể bảo vệ một thứ gì đó cổ xưa nếu họ hành động sớm và giữ bình tĩnh. Một cái cây có vẻ là điều bé nhỏ, nhưng một con phố thiếu nó sẽ không bao giờ có cảm giác như xưa.

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the best-written sentence about the threat.
options: They planned to cut it down to make the road more wide.
They planned cut it down to make the road wider.
They planned to cut it down to make the road wider.
They planned to cut down it to make the road wider.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Planned to cut it down' uses the infinitive and correct pronoun placement, with comparative 'wider'. 'More wide' is wrong; 'planned cut' drops 'to'; 'cut down it' misplaces the pronoun.
mcQuestions.hint: Where does the pronoun 'it' go in 'cut ___ down'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Pick the sentence with the correct comparative.
options: They wanted to make the road widest.
They wanted to make the road more wider.
They wanted to make the road wide more.
They wanted to make the road wider.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Wider' is the comparative of 'wide'. 'Widest' is superlative; 'more wider' is a double comparative; 'wide more' is wrong order.
mcQuestions.hint: What is the simple comparative of 'wide'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'after' to order events correctly?
options: After one of them knocked on every door, more people came outside.
After one of them knock on every door, more people came outside.
After one of them knocked on every door, more people come outside.
After one of them knocked in every door, more people came outside.
mcQuestions.explanation: Both verbs are past ('knocked', 'came') and 'on' is the right preposition. 'Knock' lacks tense; 'come' breaks agreement; 'in every door' is wrong.
mcQuestions.hint: Do you knock 'on' a door or 'in' a door?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the sentence that uses 'so' for the result.
options: We did not want to lose the tree, so that we decided to act together.
We did not want to lose the tree, so we decided to act together.
We did not want to lose the tree, so we decided act together.
We did not want to lose the tree, because we decided to act together.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'So we decided to act' shows the result, with 'to act'. 'So that' implies purpose; 'decided act' drops 'to'; 'because' reverses cause and result.
mcQuestions.hint: Does not wanting to lose it cause the decision, or the reverse?

mcQuestions.prompt: Pick the sentence that states their belief with 'because'.
options: We wrote a letter because of we believed the tree was worth saving.
We wrote a letter because we believed the tree was worth save.
We wrote a letter because we believed the tree was worth saving.
We wrote a letter, because we believing the tree was worth saving.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Worth saving' uses the gerund and 'because' joins a clause. 'Because of' needs a noun; 'worth save' is wrong; 'believing' lacks an auxiliary.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence about the older memory is best written?
options: Her mother has played under the same branches as a child.
Her mother had play under the same branches as a child.
Her mother played had under the same branches as a child.
Her mother had played under the same branches as a child.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past perfect 'had played' shows an action before the past storytelling moment. Present perfect shifts to now; 'had play' is wrong; the last has broken word order.
mcQuestions.hint: The playing happened long before the story moment — which past form fits?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the sentence about the council's decision.
options: After two meetings, they agreed to change the design.
After two meetings, they agreed change the design.
After two meetings, they agreed changing the design.
After two meetings, they agreed to changing the design.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Agreed to change' uses the infinitive. 'Agreed change' drops 'to'; 'agreed changing' and 'to changing' use the wrong form.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'agreed', which form follows: 'to change' or 'changing'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Pick the sentence about the lasting result.
options: The tree is still stand today.
The tree is still standing today.
The tree is still stood today.
The tree still is standing today.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Is still standing' uses the present continuous for an ongoing state, with 'still' before the main verb. 'Stand' and 'stood' are wrong forms; the last misplaces 'still'.
mcQuestions.hint: Which form shows the tree continuing to stand right now?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence states the lesson with a conditional naturally?
options: A small group can protect something old if they will act early.
A small group can protects something old if they act early.
A small group can protect something old if they act early.
A small group can protect something old if they acting early.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present simple after 'if' fits a general truth, and 'can protect' uses the base verb. 'Will act' after 'if' is wrong; 'protects' disagrees with the modal; 'acting' lacks an auxiliary.
mcQuestions.hint: For a general truth, what tense goes after 'if'?

options: A street without it will never feel the same.
A street without it would never feels the same.
A street without it would never felt the same.
A street without it would never feel the same.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Would never feel' fits the hypothetical idea with the base verb. 'Will' is too definite; 'feels' and 'felt' are the wrong forms after 'would'.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'would never', which form of 'feel' is correct?

criticalThinkingQuestion: When something old stands in the way of something new and useful, like a wider road, how should a community decide what to keep and what to give up?
